TSMC, the world’s leading contract chipmaker, said its A13 process will enter production in 2029, driven by strong AI demand. The node will deliver ~6% area reduction plus better efficiency and performance vs. A14 (mass production in 2028), with full design compatibility for easy migration. 
The company also unveiled A12 (an enhanced A14 platform) for 2029 and N2U (2nm variant) for 2028, targeting AI, HPC, and mobile. Advanced nanometer nodes remain key to enabling high-performance, energy-efficient AI chips. 
TSMC, whose clients include Nvidia, Apple, and AMD, continues to dominate advanced foundry (74% of Q1 revenue). It does not plan to adopt ASML’s costly next-gen high-NA EUV machines, opting instead to extend current EUV capabilities while managing rising costs. 
After posting ~$18.5B in quarterly profit, TSMC expects up to $56B in capex this year to support global expansion, especially in the U.S. and Japan.

$IREN this is the kind of second-order thinking most of the market is still missing. 
 
Everyone focuses on models. Few focus on who controls the compute — and more importantly, the incentives behind it. 
 
Anthropic’s core risk isn’t capability — it’s dependency. 
 
Relying on hyperscalers (think $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT) creates a subtle but real conflict: 
they’re both supplier and competitor. 
 
A neutral infrastructure partner: 
• no competing foundation model 
• aligned incentive = sell compute, not win the model race 
• faster deployment without political friction 
 
It doesn’t fully solve Anthropic’s dependency, but it diversifies leverage — and that’s strategic. 
 
Market still pricing IREN like a legacy HPC / energy story. 
 
But if it becomes a preferred independent compute layer for AI labs trying to avoid hyperscaler influence… 
 
That multiple doesn’t stay where it is.

Everyone focuses on models. Few focus on who controls the compute — and more importantly, the incentives behind it. 
 
Anthropic’s core risk isn’t capability — it’s dependency. 
 
Relying on hyperscalers (think $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT) creates a subtle but real conflict: 
they’re both supplier and competitor. 
 
A neutral infrastructure partner: 
• no competing foundation model 
• aligned incentive = sell compute, not win the model race 
• faster deployment without political friction 
 
It doesn’t fully solve Anthropic’s dependency, but it diversifies leverage — and that’s strategic. 
 
Market still pricing IREN like a legacy HPC / energy story. 
 
But if it becomes a preferred independent compute layer for AI labs trying to avoid hyperscaler influence… 
 
That multiple doesn’t stay where it is.

Panama has issued a new decree regulating ride-hailing platforms such as Uber, classifying them as “luxury taxis” and placing them under the supervision of the country’s Land Transit Authority . 
The regulation changes how the services operate, including tighter control over fares, which will no longer be determined purely by supply and demand but reviewed based on service conditions, costs, and comfort levels. 
Platforms will be limited to acting as intermediaries and cannot own or manage vehicles. Drivers must be Panamanian citizens with valid licenses, clean legal and traffic records, and affiliation to authorized transport organizations. 
Vehicles must meet minimum standards such as proper maintenance, air conditioning, platform identification, and payment flexibility. Non-compliance will result in fines and penalties for unauthorized transport activity.
